risks.title: Construction has plateaued while the pipeline balloons: Year-1 opens onto a digestion trough the 9% trend averages away
risks.narrative: EMEA capacity under construction has plateaued at ~2.5 GW even as the development pipeline swelled 25% to nearly 15 GW — projects are stacking in planning, not converting to builds, because grid, permits and capex phasing bind. PDU purchase orders follow construction fit-out, not pipeline announcements. And with public cloud providers taking 61% of 2025 EMEA take-up, three or four hyperscaler capex committees synchronize the whole cycle: if 2026 is an AI-capex digestion year, CE fit-out starts pause for 2–4 quarters exactly inside the venture's window while the 2030 CAGR remains intact on paper. The 1%-of-SAM Year-1 anchor implicitly assumes an on-trend year; the conversion data says the window may be a below-trend one.

mechanism.text: EMEA under-construction capacity plateaued at ~2.5 GW while the pipeline grew >25% to ~15 GW — the planning-to-construction conversion, not demand, is the binding constraint

Public cloud accounts for ~61% of EMEA take-up, so a synchronized capex digestion by a handful of buyers stalls CE fit-out starts for multiple quarters; PDU orders are placed at fit-out, so Year-1 order flow decouples from the 8–10% trend consensus that the model treats as context
obtainableFactor's 1%-of-SAM anchor presumes an average demand year; a trough year roughly halves the achievable Year-1 pace without changing TAM or SAM at all

risks.whyMissable: The CAGR node is triangulated at 8–10% across three publishers and looks solid — but annual lumpiness never appears in a CAGR. The under-construction plateau statistic sits in a real-estate H2 update, not in any PDU market report, and the model has no node representing 'which year of the cycle the launch window lands in'.
risks.falsifier: Cushman & Wakefield / JLL H1-2026 EMEA prints showing under-construction capacity breaking materially above ~2.5 GW with CE construction starts rising, or hyperscaler earnings guidance re-accelerating European data-center capex for 2026.

likelihood.rationale: The plateau and buyer concentration are documented, but 'digestion year lands exactly in the launch window' is a timing call; CBRE simultaneously projects record 20% supply growth in Europe's five largest markets, so evidence points both ways. [evidence revision: The structural trigger (plateaued construction, ballooning pipeline, conversion as binding constraint) is now firmly corroborated by multiple independent sources, strengthening the mechanism's foundation. But the timing-dependent digestion-trough claim is undercut by strong 2026-dated contradicting evidence: record hyperscaler leasing (datacenterHawk Q1 2026), record-low vacancy and 83% pre-let pipeline (JLL). High pre-let and record absorption suggest demand/fit-out is not pausing, weakening the 'below-trend Year-1' call. Net effect roughly neutral-to-slightly-lower, so a modest reduction from 0.35.]

perturbation.note: Trough-year order flow: achievable Year-1 pace at half the on-trend anchor

risks.title: The 1% Year-1 anchor is a 36-month accrual read as an annual snapshot — the pre-launch clock makes even that generous
risks.narrative: obtainableFactor's 1% was 'corrected' in refinement cycle 1 and now looks conservative and triangulated. But the benchmark corpus it leans on defines SOM as a 1–3 year (Prospeo, IdeaPlan) or 3–5 year (Wise) cumulative capture, not a Year-1 run-rate, and the one worked example in the corpus lands at 0.1% of SAM — 5x below the band's own floor. Layer the venture's actual clock on top: it is pre-launch, so months 1–6 are certification, listing and pipeline-building, not selling. A 'conservative ramp' that books its first PO in month 7–9 doesn't earn 1% of SAM; it earns a fraction of the back half of the year. The band's low edge (0.5%) is not the pessimistic case — it is the optimistic reading of the sources' own semantics.

mechanism.text: The Prospeo/IdeaPlan/Wise benchmark family defines the 1–5% obtainable share as accruing over 1–3 or 3–5 years, not per-year; the ledger pins 1% strictly to Year 1

The only concrete worked example in the corpus yields SOM/SAM ≈ 0.1% ($3.2M/$3.2B) for years 1–2, and the Tractian comparable started at $200K — not the ~$1.2M first-year figure the ledger's crossCheck asserts
The venture is pre-launch with a conservative-ramp profile: the selling window inside Year 1 is 6–9 months at best, so any annualized benchmark must be pro-rated before it is applied

mechanism.text: obtainableFactor at 0.01 overstates the realistic Year-1 capture by 2–3x; the defensible point sits at or below the band floor

risks.falsifier: A comparable early-stage DC-hardware entrant (intelligent PDU or adjacent rack power gear) documented capturing ≥1% of its serviceable market within its first 12 calendar months from launch.

likelihood.rationale: Three of four benchmark sources in the digest explicitly frame the percentage as multi-year, and the single worked example sits 5x below the band floor; the offsetting uncertainty is that the venture may have pre-launch pipeline the model doesn't credit. [evidence revision: The multi-year framing (the hypothesis's core trigger premise) is now corroborated by four independent statements in the benchmark family, strengthening the mechanism's first step. However, the more aggressive quantitative claims — the 0.1% worked example, the pro-rating of the selling window, and the specific 2–3x overstatement — were not independently corroborated in these results, and the pre-launch pipeline offset remains genuinely uncertain. The falsifier was not met (no documented ≥1% first-year capture found), which modestly supports the hypothesis but only via absence rather than affirmative evidence. Net: a bounded upward nudge from 0.5 to 0.58.]

risks.title: The declared 'large operators' beachhead is pre-signed: global framework agreements close the 40% buyer cell before the first sales call
risks.narrative: The venture's own profile names large operators as the first buyer, and the ledger prices that cell at 0.40 of PDU spend with a comfortable 0.33–0.47 band. But the band models uncertainty about SIZE, not AVAILABILITY. The procurement record shows large operators buying rack power through multi-year global framework agreements and all-in integrated contracts (the Digital Realty–Schneider $373M framework pattern; Iren's all-in Dell deal; Jülich bundling PDU into a single IT line item). A framework agreement is not a competitor to out-sell — it is a contract that removes the tender from the market for its whole term. If a majority of the 0.40 cell is framework-governed, the entrant's declared beachhead is contractually closed for exactly its Year-1 window, and the YAM is silently re-based onto the slower mid-market and channel cells.

mechanism.text: CE large operators renew or extend multi-year global rack-power framework agreements with Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton — the same vendors whose named major customers (Equinix, Telefonica, Vodafone) are the CE operator base

PDU line items ride the framework or the integrated OEM package; no standalone, contestable RFQ ever reaches the market for that operator's builds during the term — the entrant's pre-launch, first-12-months window (profile: Stage) coincides with mid-term, not renewal, for most frameworks

The addressable fraction of cust.operator-large collapses from 0.40 to roughly the framework-gap residual (~0.20); because the ledger's four channel values are unsourced internal estimates, nothing in the model currently resists this collapse

risks.whyMissable: 'Large operators buy direct' reads as good news on the founder's risk list — direct means no channel margin and a nameable account list. The surface appearance of reachability (you know exactly who to call) hides the contract structure: direct purchasing at that scale is framework purchasing, and the sensitivity band on the node models estimation error, not contractual foreclosure.
risks.falsifier: Two or more CE large operators (Equinix/NTT/Data4-class) issuing standalone, multi-vendor rack-PDU RFQs open to non-framework vendors within the next 12 months.

likelihood.rationale: Multiple documented procurement artifacts (Digital Realty–Schneider framework, Iren all-in contract, Jülich bundled line item) all point the same direction; what is uncertain is the fraction of CE operator spend covered, not the mechanism's existence. [evidence revision: Evidence strongly corroborates the prevalence and multi-year nature of framework/supply-capacity agreements among large operators (trigger and propagation mechanisms are real). But the load-bearing claim that such agreements fully foreclose the tender is weakened by the SCA's own statement that it 'preserves flexibility for a dynamic, multi-vendor environment,' and the observed existence of open competitive PDU RFQs. The flagship artifact also covers UPS/switchgear rather than rack PDU specifically. This is a modest downward revision from 0.50: the foreclosure fraction is likely less complete than the hypothesis assumes.]

perturbation.note: Framework-governed spend removed from the addressable large-operator cell; residual is framework gaps, expiries and carve-outs
Year-1 pace slows further because the remaining open demand sits in channels the venture did not plan to enter first

risks.title: The growth the CAGR promises was electrically specified before launch day — Year 1 can only sell into the installed base
risks.narrative: Nine percent CAGR reads as a tailwind: new halls, new racks, new PDU line items every quarter of Year 1. But new-build DC electrical procurement is locked at design freeze, and with long-lead power equipment (transformers, switchgear) now booked years out, operators are locking entire electrical BOMs — PDUs riding along — far earlier than the 12–24-month window the ledger's own crossCheck uses to justify its ceiling. Every megawatt that energizes during the venture's first 12 months had its rack-power vendor chosen while the venture was still pre-launch. That leaves Year-1 demand confined to retrofit, refresh and expansion churn in the installed base — a materially smaller and slower pool than 'the market' — while the model applies its 1% to the whole SAM as if new-build spend were reachable on the venture's clock.

mechanism.text: New-build electrical BOMs for capacity energizing in the venture's Year 1 were specified at design freeze, before the venture existed as a qualified vendor; the ledger's own crossCheck concedes gear is 'locked 12–24mo before fit-out' and current long-lead conditions push lock-in earlier still

The market's growth is concentrated in exactly this pre-committed new-build pipeline (the substance of the 9% CAGR), so the contestable Year-1 pool is the installed base's refresh cycle — and what standalone PDU procurement evidence exists shows small, sometimes sole-sourced lots with 1–2 bidders, not open bake-offs

Generic obtainable-share benchmarks (1% Year 1) assume the full SAM is time-reachable; no DC-hardware-specific benchmark exists that discounts for procurement lock-in, so the model has no mechanism to encode it
obtainableFactor applied to the full SAM double-counts pre-committed new-build spend as reachable; the truly contestable Year-1 share sits at or below the band floor

risks.whyMissable: The founder's plan treats the CAGR as an accelerant and 1% as conservative precisely because the market is growing — 'we only need a tiny slice of a rising tide.' The trap is that the rising portion of the tide is the least reachable portion on a 12-month clock; the model has no node that separates committed-pipeline demand from contestable demand, so nothing in the ledger flags it.
risks.falsifier: Documented cases of CE new-build data centers running competitive rack-PDU selection within 6 months of fit-out (i.e., inside the venture's selling window), or an operator confirming PDUs are procured late as a commodity line decoupled from the electrical design freeze.

likelihood.rationale: Early electrical lock-in for new builds is well-documented industry behavior and the ledger's own crossCheck relies on a 12–24-month version of it; the offsetting uncertainty is how much PDU spend is genuinely late-cycle commodity purchasing versus BOM-committed — unquantified in every retrieved source. [evidence revision: The core premise of early electrical lock-in for new builds is now strongly corroborated by multiple independent sources (exceeding the 12-24mo window the ledger uses), which modestly strengthens the hypothesis. However, the crucial unquantified gap remains: no retrieved evidence establishes whether rack PDUs specifically ride the frozen BOM or are procured late as a commodity line — the PDU sourcing guides describe RFQ processes without timing relative to design freeze. This keeps the likelihood near coin-flip; the trigger is well-supported but the PDU-coupling link that makes it consequential is still inferential.]

perturbation.note: Year-1 capture limited to installed-base retrofit/refresh; new-build pipeline pre-committed before launch

The risk is honest about this gap in its revised likelihood rationale, which earns credit but caps the score below 4.

decisionRelevance.note: The mitigation to 'target the installed-base refresh cycle' and re-map the 2026–27 pipeline by procurement stage directly changes Year-1 GTM sequencing and product focus, and the perturbation (0.005 vs band) materially moves the revenue case. risks.title: RED cybersecurity delegated act (EU 2022/30, applicable since 1 Aug 2025): one WiFi sensor port drags the PDU into EN 18031 conformity and adds two quarters to launch
risks.narrative: If the intelligent PDU ships with any radio — WiFi management fallback, BLE commissioning, wireless environmental sensors — it is radio equipment, and since 1 August 2025 the Radio Equipment Directive's cybersecurity delegated act applies in full. Conformity runs against EN 18031-1/-2, which are harmonized only WITH RESTRICTIONS: where the restrictions bite (e.g., password/access-control configurations), self-declaration is unavailable and a notified body must issue an EU-type examination certificate. For a pre-launch team this is not a 2027 problem like the CRA — it is a today problem that gates the very first shipment. The obtainableFactor's 1% Year-1 anchor already sits on a corrected, fragile benchmark; a 4–6 month notified-body queue on top of an already pre-let, slow-procurement market compresses the sellable window from 12 months to 6–8.

mechanism.text: The launch configuration includes a radio (wireless sensors / BLE commissioning), pulling the product under the RED cybersecurity delegated act — in force since 1 Aug 2025, i.e., before the venture's first shipment

EN 18031's harmonization restrictions force notified-body involvement for common access-control configurations; certificate lead time lands mid-window, and the market's own procurement clock (gear locked 12–24+ months pre-fit-out, few contestable standalone tenders) means a late start forfeits most Year-1 bake-offs
The Year-1 obtainable share, whose 1% value already rests on a benchmark-period correction and a shaky Tractian comparable, is cut roughly in half by a shortened selling window

risks.whyMissable: RED reads as a law about radios, and this is a power-distribution company; the risk hides in an accessory feature (a wireless sensor accessory or BLE setup app) that the electrical engineering team considers trivial. The compliance plan covers LVD/EMC/EN 62368-1 for the PDU itself; nobody owns the delegated act triggered by the cheapest component on the BOM.
risks.falsifier: Confirmation that the launch SKU is wired-only (no radio in any shipping configuration), or an EN 18031 assessment showing the chosen access-control design falls entirely within the harmonized (self-declaration) scope with certificate in hand before first customer ship.

likelihood.rationale: Conditional on product configuration: wireless sensing/commissioning is common in current intelligent-PDU designs, and the EN 18031 restriction traps are documented, but a wired-only launch SKU fully avoids the gate. [evidence revision: Regulatory premises are fully corroborated (timing, restrictions, NB requirement, 12–20 week lead time), strengthening confidence in the mechanism conditional on a radio-bearing SKU. Modest upward revision from 0.35 to 0.40, but the dominant residual uncertainty — whether the launch SKU actually ships with any radio and whether the chosen access-control design triggers a restriction — remains unresolved by the evidence, so the increase is bounded.]

perturbation.note: Selling window compressed from 12 to ~6–8 months by notified-body lead time

risks.title: The firmware/security audit gate precedes every intelligent-PDU listing — and it isn't in the funnel
risks.narrative: An intelligent PDU is a networked device sitting on the management LAN of critical infrastructure. Before any large CE operator issues a PO, the vendor must clear a security prequalification that a dumb-metal vendor never faces: vulnerability disclosure process, signed-firmware update infrastructure, SBOM, penetration evidence, increasingly IEC 62443-style artifacts as the EU Cyber Resilience Act obligations phase in. This gate is serial with — not parallel to — CE/TÜV product certification, and it is audited against infrastructure the pre-launch venture has to build during the very year YAM assumes it is harvesting. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut names only the classic cert stack; the security gate is a second, slower door in front of the same customers, and it opens per-operator, not per-product.

mechanism.text: Large-operator prequalification for networked power devices requires an auditable firmware update/PSIRT/SBOM capability before AVL listing; the documented EU entry stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, FCC) is already broader than the ledger's derivation names, and cyber requirements sit on top of it

A pre-launch entrant builds this infrastructure and passes per-operator audits inside the same 12 months YAM covers; each audit cycle runs one to two quarters, and the primary-buyer choice (large operators) selects exactly the buyers with the heaviest security gates
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

mechanism.text: The gate serially delays the first PO (compressing the effective selling window inside obtainableFactor) and forecloses security-gated accounts entirely for Year 1 (a reach shortfall inside serviceableFactor) — neither effect appears in the static 0.55 or the 1%

risks.whyMissable: The founder's risk list has a 'certifications' line item, and it looks handled — CE/TÜV are known, scheduled, budgeted. The security audit is invisible from the outside because it is buyer-side process, not published standard: it appears for the first time as a 40-page vendor questionnaire attached to the first serious RFQ, after the model's clock has already started.
risks.falsifier: Two or more large CE operators (or their integrators) issuing rack-PDU RFQs in the venture's first two quarters with no firmware-security/SBOM prequalification requirement, or confirming that product CE marking alone suffices for AVL listing.

likelihood.rationale: Security prequalification for networked infrastructure devices is standard practice at hyperscale/large-colo buyers and CRA obligations are phasing in, but mid-market and distributor channels gate far less strictly; probability reflects the venture's explicit large-operator-first choice. [evidence revision: Evidence corroborates the load-bearing premise that networked power devices face an operator-driven security prequalification gate (NERC vendor artifacts, SE's 62443/SBOM incumbency, Eaton's 'non-negotiable' security framing) separate from classic CE/TÜV certification. This modestly raises confidence in the gate's existence. However, the CRA's binding manufacturer obligations apply from Dec 2027 — not the near-term window — so the regulatory phase-in is less of an immediate serial delay than the narrative implies, and mid-market/distributor buyers gate far less strictly. Net small upward revision within bounds.]

risks.title: The industrial-hardware acceptance base rate un-books a third of whatever Year-1 obtains
risks.narrative: Whatever tiny share the venture obtains in year 1 will be obtained as pilots and first deployments — the profile's own 'conservative ramp' guarantees it. The reference class puts a number on what happens next: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems are never fully accepted — customers sign, the vendor deploys, and six months later the gear comes back out. Intelligent PDUs are acutely exposed because acceptance hinges on post-deployment behavior: DCIM integration, metering accuracy, firmware stability under the operator's monitoring stack. The model's obtainableFactor is a gross booking rate; nothing in the funnel converts it to net-of-reversal, and for a first-year entrant the reversal doesn't just cut revenue — it destroys the reference accounts that the Year-2 3% benchmark silently assumes exist.

mechanism.text: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems in the reference class were never fully accepted and required uninstallation — a measured base rate, not an anecdote

A pre-launch entrant's first-12-month wins are structurally pilots/POCs with acceptance clauses; intelligent-PDU acceptance depends on DCIM compatibility and metering behavior that only surfaces after deployment, in mid/enterprise accounts where the venture has no support track record

obtainableFactor 0.01 is a gross capture rate; net of the acceptance-failure base rate, realized Year-1 share runs ~30% lower, and the staged benchmark's Year-2 step (3%) presumes surviving reference accounts that reversals eliminate

risks.whyMissable: Acceptance risk hides inside a revenue number that looks conservative already — at 1% of SAM, no one audits whether the 1% stays booked. The surface appearance of prudence (a small number) conceals that the number is gross, and the funnel has no node where post-deployment reversal could even be expressed.
risks.falsifier: The venture's first three CE pilot deployments passing formal customer acceptance within 90 days of install, with no rollback or return clause exercised in the following two quarters.

likelihood.rationale: The 30% non-acceptance figure is a single-source base rate from adjacent industrial hardware, not PDU-specific; PDUs are simpler than full industrial systems, but DCIM-integration acceptance criteria and zero support track record cut the other way. [evidence revision: The trigger's 30% non-acceptance base rate is now corroborated by the exact primary source plus two independent industrial-IoT datasets, strengthening the mechanism's first step beyond a single anecdote. The mechanism's premise that acceptance hinges on post-deployment integration is reinforced. Offsetting factors from falsifying evidence — that intelligent-PDU pilots do succeed, but predominantly for incumbents with support track records — reinforce rather than weaken the specific worry about a zero-track-record entrant. Modest upward revision from 0.35 to 0.40, bounded because the base rate remains from adjacent classes (robotics, IoT, AI) rather than PDU-specific, and PDUs are simpler hardware.]

perturbation.note: Gross Year-1 capture haircut by the industrial-hardware non-acceptance base rate (~30% of deployments reverse)

risks.title: Cyber Resilience Act (Reg. EU 2024/2847): the conformity clock for a networked PDU, started today, ends after the Year-1 window closes
risks.narrative: An intelligent PDU is a 'product with digital elements' under the CRA. Vulnerability-handling and 24h ENISA incident-reporting obligations apply from 11 September 2026 and full conformity (CE marking against CRA essential requirements, SBOM, coordinated vulnerability disclosure, support-period declaration) from 11 December 2027 — squarely overlapping a pre-launch venture's first 12 months. The harmonized standards CEN/CENELEC must deliver are not yet published, and remote-management functionality risks classifying the product as an 'important product' requiring third-party assessment through a notified-body queue that does not yet exist at scale. Worse, data-center buyers front-run: because PDUs installed in 2026 live in racks past 2027, procurement specs are already demanding CRA-readiness attestations today. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut was built on the old CE/TÜV/ecodesign stack; it prices none of this, and the obtainableFactor's 1% Year-1 pace assumes the product can be quoted into 2026 tenders at all.

mechanism.text: CRA obligations land inside the entry window: reporting duties from Sept 2026, full conformity Dec 2027, with harmonized standards unpublished and notified-body capacity unbuilt — a fixed regulatory calendar colliding with a pre-launch stage

Large operators and colos, whose gear outlives the 2027 deadline, insert CRA-readiness clauses into 2026 RFQs; the existing cert-stack rationale for serviceableFactor ('justifies <1.0 but doesn't quantify 0.55') already shows the model treats compliance as a static haircut, not a moving gate

Year-1 tenders that are contestable at all are already scarce and pre-locked 12–24+ months ahead; adding a certification artifact the entrant cannot yet produce removes it from the shortlists that make up the 1% anchor
Reach shrinks (CRA-gated demand exits the serviceable pool) and pace slows (Year-1 obtainable share falls below the 1% anchor while incumbents ship CRA-ready roadmaps)

risks.whyMissable: 'CE marking' is already a checked box on the founder's compliance list, and the CRA presents itself as just another CE regulation. The trap is procedural, not substantive: the conformity ROUTE changes (SBOM, vulnerability process, possible third-party assessment) and buyers enforce it via procurement one to two years before the legal deadline — so the risk bites in 2026 sales cycles while the founder's calendar says 'deadline 2027'.
risks.falsifier: Three 2026 CE data-center PDU RFQs (operator or colo) whose specification documents contain no CRA/cyber-conformity or SBOM requirement, plus confirmation that networked PDUs fall in the CRA default (self-assessment) class.

likelihood.rationale: The CRA dates and product scope are enacted law, not forecast; residual uncertainty is only the important-product classification of remote-management PDUs and how aggressively CE buyers front-run the 2027 deadline in 2026 specs. [evidence revision: The regulatory trigger (dates, scope, in-scope status of networked PDUs) is firmly corroborated as enacted law, keeping the core mechanism plausible. But two sub-premises weakened: (1) the 'notified-body queue that does not yet exist' and 'harmonized standards unpublished' argument is softened because Implementing Regulation 2025/2392 (Nov 2025) has fixed technical classifications, and (2) evidence indicates PDUs likely fall in the ~90% default self-assessment class rather than 'important product' third-party assessment — Annex III Class I lists network management systems/routers/switches but not PDUs. The procurement front-running claim ('2026 RFQs demanding CRA-readiness today') is asserted by compliance vendors but not directly evidenced in any actual PDU spec (the Vertiv spec cites only UL/FCC/IEEE, no CRA). Net: strong on the calendar, weaker on the escalation and front-running severity, so slight downward revision from 0.55 to 0.50.]

Year-1 pace falls below the 1% anchor as 2026 shortlists require CRA-readiness

risks.title: Near-zero-margin PDU bundling inside power+cooling packages means the contestable Year-1 pool is below the model's own 0.5% floor
risks.narrative: The obtainableFactor band (0.5–3%) assumes there exists a pool of standalone rack-PDU purchase decisions a new entrant can compete for. But the same incumbents sell the $23B power and $26B cooling packages those PDUs plug into, and the procurement record shows PDUs travelling as embedded line items inside those packages — where the incumbent can price the PDU at near-zero margin because the busway, UPS and cooling carry the deal. The only standalone PDU procurements surfaced by research were tiny ($58K–$130K), had 1–2 bidders, and one was explicitly sole-sourced. Meanwhile the 1%-of-SAM anchor itself is generic startup folklore: the sources define SOM as a 1–3 or 3–5 year cumulative window, and the closest worked hardware comparable runs at ~0.1% of SAM. Both legs of the 1% — the benchmark and the tender evidence — point below the band, not inside it.

mechanism.text: Incumbents holding both the power ($23B) and cooling ($26B) packages bundle intelligent PDUs into integrated deals, cross-subsidising PDU price to zero incremental margin — no bake-off is ever held on the PDU line

The residual standalone-tender pool is small and often non-contestable (sole-sourced, 1–2 bidders), while the 1% Year-1 benchmark the node anchors on is defined by its own sources as a multi-year cumulative figure — Prospeo frames SOM as 1–3 years, Wise as 3–5, and TryBuildCo's worked example lands at ~0.1% of SAM

True Year-1 obtainable share of SAM falls to ~0.3% — below the band's 0.5% floor — because the denominator (SAM) includes bundled demand that never becomes a contestable decision, and the Tractian cross-check that supported ≤1% is itself unverified against its source ($200K starting revenue, not $1.2M first-year)

risks.whyMissable: The founder's team already 'corrected' this node once (3%→1%) and the band looks conservative, so it reads as the model's most stress-tested number. The hidden failure is compositional: the band debates WHICH percentage of SAM is winnable, while the bundling mechanism removes demand from the contestable denominator entirely — a smaller pie of decisions, not a smaller slice of spend.
risks.falsifier: A logged pipeline of ≥5 standalone, multi-vendor rack-PDU tenders in CE-7, each ≥€250K, posted within any 6-month window, in which a non-incumbent vendor is qualified to bid.

likelihood.rationale: The bundling evidence (integrated contracts, sole-sourced standalone bids) and the benchmark-window semantics both push the same direction, but retrofit and colo tenant purchases do generate some genuinely standalone PDU decisions that the tender search may have under-sampled. [evidence revision: Evidence cuts both ways. The sole-sourced Zurich PDU award and spec-locked single-model tenders support the non-contestability and bundling-adjacent thesis, and the SOM-is-multi-year semantics are confirmed. But the surfaced CHF ~2M standalone PDU deal directly undercuts the 'only tiny ($58K-$130K)' claim, and the SOM sources reaffirm 1-5%/3-5% bands rather than pointing below 0.5%. The core zero-margin bundling mechanism remains unevidenced. Net effect is a marginal downward revision from 0.4 reflecting mixed corroboration.]

risks.title: A single metro's 24–36-month grid queue gates ~37% of the CE TAM's Year-1 flow
risks.narrative: Germany is 0.50 of the base and Frankfurt hosts ~75% of German IT capacity — so roughly 37% of the venture's TAM routes through one metro's grid interconnection queue, where waits now run 24–36 months and vacancy is 2.2%. The model treats DE as a diversified half of the market; in the first-12-months window it is really a bet that Frankfurt's 542 MW under construction energizes on schedule. If 2026 energizations slip right — grid queue, plus EnEfG efficiency/heat-reuse compliance adding commissioning friction — the fit-outs that would generate the venture's largest tranche of Year-1 PDU purchase orders move to 2027–28 while the 9% trend stays intact on paper.

mechanism.text: Frankfurt grid connections face 24–36-month waits while 542 MW sits under construction and 383 MW in planning — energization slots, not demand, set commissioning dates

Frankfurt is ~75% of German capacity and DE is 0.50 of the CE base, so a queue slip in one metro defers the largest single block of near-term fit-out demand; PDU POs land at fit-out, so deferred energization means deferred orders regardless of the 2030 CAGR

risks.title: Buyer concentration × procurement timing empties the Year-1 window in the exact cell the ramp assumes
risks.narrative: The 1%-of-SAM Year-1 benchmark implicitly assumes demand arrives smoothly from many independent buyers. But the venture's chosen cell — DE-heavy geography (0.50) times large operators (0.40) — is the lumpiest corner of the cube: a handful of Frankfurt/Amsterdam campus fit-outs whose electrical BoM, PDUs included, is specified at design and tender stage long before delivery. Capacity delivering in the venture's first 12 months was specced in 2024–25; capacity being specced now delivers after the window closes. The correlation between concentration and procurement timing means the truly contestable slice of SAM in months 0–12 is far below what a market-average benchmark implies — the YAM can miss even at the band's low end without a single node being 'wrong'.

mechanism.text: Year-1 demand in the target cell is dominated by a few flagship fit-outs: Frankfurt alone is a 1.2GW market and Europe's 937MW of 2025 new supply is concentrated in a handful of campuses whose electrical procurement is locked at tender, well before the venture's launch window opens

With geo.DE=0.50 and cust.operator-large=0.40, the obtainable pool is not thousands of independent purchase decisions but a short list of lumpy, pre-committed projects — 85% of European take-up flows through FLAPD-scale operators whose specs are set upstream — so the smooth-arrival assumption behind the 1%-of-SAM benchmark fails precisely in this cell

obtainableFactor 0.01 is a market-average Year-1 benchmark applied to a cell whose months-0–12 contestable share approaches zero; YAM is overstated for this specific geography×buyer mix even inside the stated 0.005–0.03 band

But CBRE shows European colo take-up being outstripped by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenant demand — and in wholesale/prelet halls the tenant, not the operator, dictates the rack power architecture. If hyperscale tenants specify busway plus power shelves inside leased halls (the same migration the exemplar tracks in self-build), the colo segment's PDU content leaves the category through the tenant spec sheet while the operator's capacity numbers keep growing 19% a year. The one segment the engine treats as certain is the one with the untracked contagion path.

risks.title: The DE×hyperscale anchor cell is a mirage: CE-7's hyperscale-owned share is far below the rescaled global 44%
risks.narrative: seg.hyperscale rescales Synergy's global 48% flat onto Central Europe. But ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7. Inside CE, hyperscale presence is mostly leased capacity inside Frankfurt and Amsterdam colo campuses, which Statista's 'colocation and scale colocation' figure already counts on the geography axis as colo MW. The cube therefore double-books the same megawatts — hyperscale on the segment axis, colo on the geo axis — and the implied beachhead cell (DE 0.50 × hyperscale 0.44 ≈ 22% of TAM as 'German hyperscale') largely does not exist as hyperscale-owned facilities. The real buyer of that capacity's PDUs is the colo operator or its integrator, with colo-grade requirements: tenant billing metering, multi-tenant DCIM. A product roadmap and sales motion built for hyperscale custom SKUs in Germany aims at a cell the market never populated.

mechanism.text: Synergy's 48% hyperscale share is global; CBRE-sourced reporting places ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build in Ireland, Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — a geography that barely overlaps CE-7 — while the model applies the global split flat with a 0.91 carve-out

Within CE-7, hyperscale demand is predominantly leased: European colocation remains significantly larger than self-build, FLAPD colo hubs (Frankfurt, Amsterdam in CE) absorb the hyperscale take-up, and the Statista geo denominator counts that same 'scale colocation' MW as colo — so the segment axis and geo axis book the same capacity to different segments

seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates CE hyperscale-owned demand and understates the colo-operator cell; the venture's implied DE×hyperscale×large-operator beachhead is materially smaller, and the real buyers need colo-grade product (billing metering, multi-tenant DCIM), not hyperscale custom builds
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: seg.hyperscale is the best-dressed node on the segment axis — 'triangulated' across multiple Synergy vintages with a tight 0.40–0.48 band. The flaw is not the number but its geography: a global capacity split cannot survive contact with a region whose hyperscale capacity is leased rather than owned, and no band on a correctly-triangulated global figure covers a regional composition error.
risks.falsifier: A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (Synergy, CBRE or JLL) showing hyperscale-owned/self-build capacity ≥40% of CE-7 total — i.e., the global split does transfer.

likelihood.rationale: The 60%-outside-CE self-build concentration is directly sourced, and Frankfurt's status as a colo (not self-build) hub is well documented; residual uncertainty is how much leased hyperscale capacity should still be attributed to the hyperscale buyer for PDU-spec purposes. [evidence revision: The core geographic concentration premise is now directly corroborated by the CBRE-sourced DCD article (verbatim match to the hypothesis), strengthening confidence that CE-7 hyperscale self-build is well below the rescaled global share. Synergy confirms ~half of hyperscale capacity is leased, and Structure Research shows Frankfurt's hyperscale footprint is largely 'hyperscale colocation.' Residual uncertainty remains around how much leased hyperscale capacity should still be attributed to the hyperscale buyer for PDU-spec purposes (Synergy's wholesale-colocation tracker explicitly splits out a 'Hyperscale' segment within wholesale colo, suggesting the buyer/spec question is genuinely ambiguous rather than clearly favoring the colo operator). Modest upward revision from 0.4 to 0.5.]

perturbation.note: Hyperscale-owned CE share corrected for the self-build concentration outside CE-7
Mix-shift: leased 'scale colocation' capacity re-attributed to the colo-operator segment where the PDU purchasing decision actually sits

risks.title: The OEM/integrator cell is squeezed shut from both sides: brand-spec deals go to incumbent ecosystems, white-label seats go to scale ODMs
risks.narrative: The 0.22 OEM/integrator cell looks like the natural entry channel for an industrial entrant — and superficially the evidence agrees, because BOTH routes into it are documented: integrators buy branded PDUs, and buyer's guides advertise OEM/private-label options. The trap is that each route is closed for a different reason. Every integrator case study found shows selection of branded incumbents on certification, DCIM compatibility and support ecosystems — assets a Year-1 entrant does not have. The white-label seats, meanwhile, are held by Asian ODMs (the Digipower pattern) competing on a manufacturing cost curve a Central European industrial entrant cannot match. The cell's 0.22 value is an unsourced internal estimate with an addressability premise no source supports; for this venture the genuinely open fraction may be a third of the modeled cell.

mechanism.text: Rack integrators select PDU brands on features, certification and DCIM-ecosystem compatibility — the documented integrator behavior favors incumbents whose monitoring stacks are already deployed — while the private-label demand that does exist is served by dedicated OEM/ODM manufacturers with global scale

An industrial entrant sits in the dead zone between the two: no installed DCIM/support ecosystem to win brand-spec designs (those accrue to the Legrand/Raritan/Server Technology axis and the top-5 vendor set) and no ODM cost position to win price-spec white-label seats

Addressable share of cust.oem falls from 0.22 to ~0.08 (the fraction of integrator demand that is regional, spec-flexible, and not price-anchored to ODM quotes); the funnel's channel mix shifts further onto cells that are themselves contested

risks.whyMissable: The cell appears doubly open — 'we can sell branded OR white-label' — which is exactly why it survives founder scrutiny. Two documented routes in feels like redundancy; it is actually two separately-owned gates, and the evidence that each gate is held (integrator brand loyalty; ODM incumbency) sits in different source types that never appear on the same page of a risk review.
risks.falsifier: One CE rack integrator or OEM signing a design-in or white-label agreement with the entrant, or a documented integrator RFQ in which a non-incumbent, non-ODM intelligent PDU is evaluated on merit within the launch year.

likelihood.rationale: Every retrieved integrator case study shows incumbent-brand selection and no source quantifies an open OEM share, but the sample of case studies is small and integrator behavior for differentiated intelligent-PDU features is untested — judgment on how far the pattern generalizes. [evidence revision: Evidence solidly corroborates both qualitative prongs of the squeeze mechanism — documented ODM white-label supplier and multiple incumbent-brand+DCIM integrator selections, with no falsifying design-in found. This modestly strengthens confidence in the direction of the effect. But the load-bearing quantitative claim (0.22→0.08) remains entirely unsourced, and the sample is still small case studies that do not test how differentiated intelligent-PDU features fare in a merit-based regional RFQ, so the increase from 0.4 is bounded and small.]

perturbation.note: Only the regional, spec-flexible integrator fraction remains addressable; brand-spec and ODM-price fractions foreclosed

risks.title: Distributor line-cards and integrator qualification run on annual calendars the 12-month plan cannot intersect
risks.narrative: 35% of the modeled funnel flows through OEM/integrator (0.22) and distributor (0.13) cells — both admitted internal estimates with zero evidence attached. The reference class says both channels have a calendar: distributor line-card reviews are annual events requiring certifications, stock commitments and demand proof in hand; integrators demonstrably select branded incumbents on certification, DCIM compatibility and support (every case study found — Vertiv/Greenergy, Legrand/MIND Park — shows brand selection, none shows a new entrant winning a slot). A pre-launch vendor with no certs on day 0 misses the current listing cycle entirely and enters the next one as an unproven applicant. Within the YAM window these two cells are not small — they are closed, and the model treats 'closed this year' as indistinguishable from 'open at 35%'.

mechanism.text: Distributor line-card onboarding is an annual-cycle gate requiring completed certifications and reference installs at application time — assets a pre-launch entrant does not possess in month 1 and cannot assemble before the cycle closes

The parallel OEM/integrator path is gated the same way but on brand: integrators select PDU suppliers on certification, DCIM compatibility and support track record, a pattern consistent across the corpus's integrator case studies and the incumbent vendor sets that dominate every published landscape

cust.oem (0.22) and cust.distributor (0.13) together carry 35% of the funnel into SAM, but within the first 12 months both cells are calendar-foreclosed; the SAM the Year-1 obtainable percentage is applied to is materially smaller than modeled

risks.whyMissable: Channel shares look like a mix question — 'we'll get some of each' — so the risk review debates the percentages, not their availability. The hidden feature is that indirect channels are not continuously open markets: they are gated queues with annual admission, and a pre-launch company applies to the queue rather than selling into the cell. Nothing in a static share number signals that the cell has an opening date.
risks.falsifier: A signed CE master-distributor listing or OEM/integrator supply agreement with committed volumes, effective within the venture's first six months.

likelihood.rationale: Annual line-card cycles and brand-based integrator selection are standard for the reference class (industrial electrical hardware entering EU distribution); uncertainty is whether an aggressive pre-launch certification push can compress one cycle, which some Asian PDU entrants have managed with EU-stock commitments. [evidence revision: Core gating premise (certification/brand-based channel selection) is corroborated, but the strict 'annual-calendar-foreclosed within 12 months' amplifier is contradicted by real examples of new entrants signing distribution/framework agreements effective in-year. These are branded/established firms (UPS, networking) rather than pre-launch no-cert PDU startups, so the falsifier is not fully met — but the existence of near-term distribution entry paths reduces the probability that these cells are entirely closed in Year 1. Modest downward revision from 0.4 to 0.33.]

perturbation.note: Distributor cell unavailable within the YAM window — listing cycle cannot complete inside 12 months from a zero-cert start
Integrator cell half-foreclosed: brand/certification selection excludes the entrant from established integrator programs; residual is opportunistic white-label volume

risks.title: Global framework agreements dissolve the 'large operator direct' cell the venture plans to enter first
risks.narrative: The funnel counts 40% of PDU spend as 'large operators' — the venture's declared first buyer. But the number is a capacity-share proxy, not a channel reality. The documented procurement pattern for exactly these buyers is HQ-level global frameworks and integrator bundles: pan-European colos and hyperscalers sign multi-year power-chain packages with Schneider/Vertiv-class suppliers or take PDUs embedded in OEM/ODM rack builds. A regional entrant never sees an RFP for that spend. The cell exists as consumption; it barely exists as a contestable transaction. The independence assumption geography × segment × buyer hides that the biggest buyer cell and the least reachable procurement route are the same cell.

mechanism.text: Large operators consolidate rack-power procurement into global frameworks and integrated OEM/ODM packages: Vertiv's named major customers span Equinix and global telecoms, PDU vendors explicitly market OEM/private-label channels, and ODMs like Digipower supply operators via white-label design programs

The 0.40 cell was derived from 'hyperscale+colo ≈ 62% of capacity and large operators buy direct' — a consumption share, not a channel split; the only attached datapoint (30% of one supplier's orders) measures DC exposure, not who signs the PO. Spend transacted under frameworks negotiated outside CE is recorded in this cell but is foreclosed to a pre-launch regional entrant

cust.operator-large 0.40 overstates the discrete-buyer share of PDU spend; the funnel keeps 40% of TAM inside the venture's first channel while the real contestable route runs through OEM/integrator packages — a different sell with different economics
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: The buyer-mix row reads as a demand split, and 'large operators buy direct' matches sales intuition. The ledger flags the node 'needs-source', which reads as a precision problem to fix with interviews — not a reachability problem. The number can be right about who consumes PDUs and still wrong about who awards the contract.
risks.falsifier: Three CE large operators confirming they run discrete, vendor-open rack-PDU RFPs (not framework call-offs or integrator bundles) for their 2026 fit-outs.

likelihood.rationale: Every procurement case surfaced in the corpus (framework agreements, all-in integrated contracts, bundled IT line items) points toward embedded procurement; no counterexample of a discrete open large-operator PDU tender in CE was found. Uncertainty remains on what fraction of the cell is framework-covered. [evidence revision: Strong independent corroboration that large operators lock power-chain spend into multi-year framework/SCA and OEM/ODM bundle channels raises confidence in the mechanism's core premise. But two mitigating signals — Digital Realty's explicit 'dynamic, multi-vendor environment' clause and a Dutch PDU maker selling directly to leading operators — plus the absence of any CE-specific evidence (the marquee SCAs are US deals) cap the upward revision. Net modest increase from 0.45 to 0.50.]

Mix-shift: that spend re-appears in the OEM/integrator channel, which requires a different go-to-market

risks.title: The best-triangulated geography is the trap: NL's 28% sits on a capped colo base while its growth migrates to self-build architectures a regional entrant can't quote into
risks.narrative: geo.NL is the model's most reassuring node — Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But that triangulation validates yesterday's colocation stock, not the composition of forward demand. The Netherlands is one of four countries holding ~60% of Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity, and self-build is growing 24% YoY versus colo's 19% — while Amsterdam (≈70% of NL colo) is supply-constrained. So the venture's second-largest geography weight (28% of the base) rests on a slice where the PDU-native part is capped and the growing part procures through hyperscaler global frameworks and busway/shelf architectures that a pre-launch regional entrant cannot reach in its first 12 months. The MW totals will keep rising; the biddable NL demand won't.

mechanism.text: NL is among the four countries concentrating ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity, and European self-build additions grow 24% YoY — in NL, forward capacity growth increasingly happens outside the 951MW colo base that geo.NL's 0.28 apportionment measures

Self-build capacity procures power distribution through hyperscaler global specs rather than regional channels, while the colo segment — larger but slower at 19% YoY and slowly losing share of total capacity — is the only NL demand a new CE entrant can actually bid; Amsterdam supply constraints cap even that

geo.NL 0.28 overstates the entrant-reachable Dutch slice during the pre-launch first-12-months window: the ratio is anchored to a stalling colo stock while the growth it implicitly promises accrues to out-of-category, out-of-channel self-build

risks.whyMissable: geo.NL carries the ledger's cleanest triangulation (two independent sources within 3%), so it reads as the one geography nobody needs to re-examine. Agreement between sources measuring the same colo stock says nothing about whether that stock is the demand a Year-1 entrant will face — the precision of the snapshot hides the composition shift.
risks.falsifier: 2026 NL capacity-addition evidence (DDCA annual report plus fit-out tenders) showing new Dutch MW — including hyperscale campuses — specifying rack PDUs procured through channels open to regional vendors at rates proportional to NL's 28% weight.

likelihood.rationale: The self-build concentration and growth differential are directly sourced and Amsterdam's constraints are well documented; the offset is that NL retail/wholesale colo refresh demand is real and PDU-native, so the question is degree — whether reachable NL demand supports 0.28 or something closer to 0.20–0.22 of the base [evidence revision: Amsterdam supply constraints and the concentration of forward growth in hyperscale campuses are strongly corroborated, strengthening the mechanism's premises. However, the specific self-build vs colo growth differential (24% vs 19%) was not independently confirmed, and one source suggests some hyperscale/cloud rack-and-power demand may still flow through Dutch-relevant channels (e.g., Schleifenbauer domestic PDU vendor), tempering the full-trap conclusion. Modest upward revision from 0.35 to 0.40.]

perturbation.note: NL weight corrected to the colo-anchored, entrant-reachable slice; self-build growth and capped Amsterdam supply removed from the effective ratio

risks.title: The 0.91 rescale imports hyperscale that isn't in CE-7: the segment mix is misallocated even though the TAM headline doesn't move
risks.narrative: seg.hyperscale applies Synergy's global 48% hyperscale capacity share to Central Europe via a flat 0.91 carve-out. But ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7 — and Europe's self-build growth (24% YoY) keeps landing disproportionately in those markets. CE-7 is structurally colo- and enterprise-weighted relative to the global mix the model borrowed. Because segment shares sum to the same base, the TAM headline is untouched and no alarm rings; but the SAM haircut logic, certification priorities and Year-1 pipeline are all built on a buyer mix that overweights hyperscale by perhaps a third. This is a resourcing kill, not a sizing kill.

mechanism.text: Synergy's global 48% hyperscale share is transplanted to CE-7 through a flat 0.91 carve-out, but Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity is concentrated (~60%) in Ireland, NL, Sweden and Belgium — a geographic pattern that makes the global split a poor proxy for CE-7

European hyperscale self-build grows 24% YoY versus colo's 19%, and that growth keeps concentrating outside CE-7, so the divergence between the borrowed global mix and the true CE-7 mix widens through exactly the venture's entry window rather than converging

The €300M base is misallocated across segments: hyperscale overweighted, colo/enterprise underweighted. Since shares sum to the same TAM, the headline never flags the error — but which buyers to certify for, which channels to build and where Year-1 pipeline effort goes are all keyed to the wrong weights

risks.whyMissable: A wrong segment mix leaves TAM, SAM and YAM totals identical, so every number the client's team has reviewed reads the same; the tornado tests each share's band in isolation but never tests whether the mix as a whole was transplanted from the wrong geography. The 0.91 carve-out looks like a careful adjustment, which disguises that the underlying split was never Europe-scoped.
risks.falsifier: A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (CBRE, Synergy regional cut, or JLL) showing hyperscale-owned/operated capacity at ≥40% of CE-7 MW, validating the transplanted global share.

likelihood.rationale: The 60%-outside-CE concentration figure is directly sourced and the ledger's own promotion path admits no Europe-scoped split exists; the main uncertainty is how much hyperscale leased-in-colo capacity within CE offsets the self-build concentration elsewhere [evidence revision: The two source-anchored premises — the borrowed Synergy global mix and the ~60% concentration outside CE-7 plus divergent 24%/19% growth — are strongly corroborated by independent outlets and CBRE directly. This modestly raises confidence in the misallocation direction. But the residual uncertainty flagged in the original rationale (how much hyperscale leased-in-colo capacity within CE-7 offsets self-build concentration elsewhere) remains unresolved: no CE-7-scoped hyperscale share was located, so the exact magnitude of the overweight and thus whether it materially changes resourcing decisions is still inferential. A small upward revision only.]

risks.title: The €300M base counts the non-intelligent majority of a market the venture cannot sell into
risks.narrative: The venture sells intelligent rack PDUs, but tamBase anchors on Grand View's $2.81B 'rack PDU' figure — and Grand View itself states the NON-intelligent segment held the largest revenue share in 2025. IndexBox confirms the split is real by selling 'Rack PDUs' and 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' as separate EU reports. The scope-down chain carefully audited geography and FX but never audited product type: roughly half the €300M base is basic/metered units the product line does not address. TAM, SAM and YAM all inherit a ~2x product-scope inflation while the ledger label ('rack-PDU market, all segments/buyers') reads as if it were scope-matched.

mechanism.text: Grand View's own segmentation of the $2.81B anchor reports the non-intelligent rack-PDU segment held the largest 2025 revenue share, while the venture's product is intelligent PDUs only

Independent sizings of the intelligent-only subset run $2.1B (GIA 2024) to $3.0B (MRF 2025) against $1.7–3.9B for total rack-PDU — and IndexBox maintains distinct EU reports for 'Rack PDUs' vs 'Intelligent Rack PDUs', confirming the vendor community treats them as different products with different bases

The €300M CE base carries the full-category value; the venture-relevant intelligent-only base is materially smaller, and every downstream funnel stage (SAM €165M, YAM €1.65M) inherits the inflation because serviceableFactor haircuts reach, not product scope

risks.whyMissable: The label 'rack-PDU market' looks perfectly scope-matched to a rack-PDU vendor — the refinement cycle even proved its diligence by removing the PDUs+PSUs ceiling, creating the impression definitional contamination was handled. The remaining contamination sits inside the anchor's own segmentation table, one level below the headline number anyone reads.
risks.falsifier: A scope-pure European source (IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs headline, or an Omdia segment table) showing intelligent/monitored/switched PDUs at ≥75% of European rack-PDU revenue — i.e., Europe's revenue mix is intelligent-dominated even if global unit mix is not.

likelihood.rationale: The anchor source directly states non-intelligent held the largest revenue share globally; the offset is that intelligent units carry 3–5x ASPs and European colo/hyperscale buyers skew intelligent, so the revenue-weighted European intelligent share plausibly lands at 45–60% rather than below 40%

perturbation.note: Restrict the base to the intelligent-PDU-relevant revenue share of the rack-PDU category (~55% revenue-weighted in Europe)

risks.title: tamBase apportions revenue by standing MW, but PDU revenue follows new-build flow — which is concentrating outside CE-7
risks.narrative: The CE-7 = 39.8%-of-Europe hop divides standing colocation stock by standing stock. But rack-PDU revenue is fit-out-driven: it is earned when MW are delivered or refreshed, not while they sit installed. CBRE shows the flow concentrating away from CE: 57% of 2025's 937MW of new European supply lands in FLAPD (of which only Frankfurt and Amsterdam are CE-7), 85% of take-up is FLAPD, London alone exceeds 1GW and is Europe's largest market, and double-digit growth in secondary markets is going to Milan and Madrid — not Warsaw or Vienna. If CE-7's share of European capacity ADDITIONS is meaningfully below its 39.8% share of stock, the €300M systematically overstates where 2026 PDU purchase orders will actually be written, even if every input number is individually correct.

mechanism.text: European colo revenue-generating activity is flow-shaped: 937MW of new supply in 2025, 57% of it in FLAPD, and 85% of take-up in FLAPD — a distribution measurably different from the standing-stock table the CE-7 ratio is computed from

London (>1GW, Europe's largest market) and Frankfurt together represent roughly half of European supply, and CBRE's tracked secondary-growth markets are Milan and Madrid — so the growth increment that a 9% CAGR is made of accrues disproportionately to non-CE-7 geographies, while CE-7's standing stock skews toward older, lower-density halls with slower PDU refresh

The 39.8% stock ratio overstates CE-7's share of near-term PDU revenue; tamBase should be read as a stock-weighted ceiling, with the flow-weighted (order-relevant) base materially lower — a bias no band edge in the tornado captures because it is a method error, not parameter noise

risks.whyMissable: Stock apportionment is the standard top-down move and every input is sourced and recent — nothing on the page looks estimated. The rot is in an unstated physical assumption (PDU € per standing MW is uniform across regions and vintages) that no source states because no source measures it; you only see it by asking what event actually generates a PDU invoice.
risks.falsifier: CBRE's 2025–26 delivery pipeline (or Statista's 2026-vintage country deltas) showing CE-7 capturing ≥38% of new European colocation MW delivered — i.e., flow share matching stock share.

likelihood.rationale: The FLAPD concentration of additions and take-up is well documented, but Frankfurt and Amsterdam are themselves FLAPD members inside CE-7, which claws back part of the flow; the net CE-7 flow deficit vs its stock share is real but plausibly 5–10 points rather than catastrophic [evidence revision: Evidence robustly confirms flow concentrates in FLAPD (57–72% of supply, ~half in London+Frankfurt, 46% of take-up in London+Frankfurt). London (non-CE-7) being ~1.3GW dominant strengthens the CE-7 flow-deficit thesis. But because Frankfurt and Amsterdam are CE-7 members and Frankfurt is itself a huge flow recipient, part of the FLAPD flow claws back into CE-7, keeping the net deficit moderate rather than catastrophic — consistent with the original 5–10 point estimate. Slight upward revision reflects strong corroboration of the directional mechanism.]

perturbation.note: Re-weight the CE-7 share from standing stock toward capacity-additions flow (~32% vs 39.8%)

risks.title: A hostile reviewer strikes the unsourced 'Europe ≈ 30%' hop and the €300M loses its floor, not just its band
risks.narrative: The tamBase derivation looks disciplined — a refinement cycle even killed a contaminated €495M ceiling. But its load-bearing hop, 'Europe ≈ 30% of global', is an inserted analyst estimate: Grand View publishes only North America's 38% and no source in any search pass gives a Europe or CE rack-PDU figure. Worse, the global anchor itself is unsettled — same-category estimates run from $1.697B to $5.3B (QY Research $3.255B and Strategic Market Research $5.3B sit in this venture's own digest), a spread wider than the model's ±20% band. A diligence partner strikes the 30%, pairs the lowest credible global anchor with a conservative Europe share, and lands near €200M — below the band's floor — and the model has no sourced fact with which to push back. The band communicates 'we've bounded the uncertainty'; the derivation chain shows the uncertainty was never bounded, only anchored to one publisher's number times an invented ratio.

mechanism.text: Diligence cross-references the global anchor: QY Research puts the global data-center PDU market at $3,255M while Strategic Market Research says $5.3B for rack PDUs alone and other publishers go as low as $1.7B — the primary anchor is one draw from a >2x distribution

With the anchor contested, the unsourced 'Europe ≈ 30%' multiplier and the Statista-based '39.8% of Europe colo MW' CE-7 step (itself scope-inflated vs CBRE's 4,726MW top-15 Europe figure) cannot rescue the chain — every hop is either single-source or inserted

tamBase's effective uncertainty is wider and skewed below the stated 240–360 band; every downstream number inherits a base that cannot survive its first hostile audit, which matters most when the model is used to raise capital or justify CE manufacturing capacity

risks.whyMissable: The refinement-cycle narrative makes the base look stress-tested — a contaminated ceiling was found and removed, which reads as rigor. But the refinement fixed the cross-check while leaving the primary chain's weakest hop (the inserted Europe share) untouched, and even the comparator used to lower the ceiling was itself drawn from a broader-category report. The audit trail creates confidence the audit never earned.
risks.falsifier: The commissioned IndexBox EU rack-PDU country tables (the ledger's own named promotion path) placing the CE-7 base at €270M or above.

likelihood.rationale: The 2.3x global anchor spread and the unsourced Europe hop are documented facts; whether the true value sits below the band floor (vs merely being unverifiable) is a judgment call — the Germany-alone $509.6M broader-PDU figure cuts the other way, so the distribution is wide in both directions but the defensibility failure is certain. [evidence revision: Evidence confirms the documented facts the hypothesis leans on — the unsourced Europe hop (GVR gives only NA 38%) and the >2x global anchor spread. This solidifies the certain 'defensibility failure.' But the remaining uncertainty — whether the true CE-7 value lands below the band floor vs. merely being unverifiable — is unchanged; the newly-surfaced IndexBox EU/country reports (the falsifier path) exist and could rescue the number, and no search returned a Europe figure below the floor. Slight upward nudge for the corroborated premises, bounded by the unresolved directional judgment.]

risks.title: Annual line reviews plus Legrand's dual-brand grip close the distributor cell mid-cycle — the entrant arrives with the shelf already allocated
risks.narrative: The 0.13 distributor cell looks like the easy channel: no framework agreements, documented master-distributor models, even explicit private-label options for distributors in buyer's guides. But distribution in this category runs on annual line reviews with exclusivity and margin-program terms, and the intelligent-PDU shelf specifically is consolidated — Legrand owns BOTH Raritan and Server Technology, the two intelligent-PDU specialist brands, giving one vendor two slots on every line card and strong leverage in review negotiations. A pre-launch entrant hits the market mid-cycle: even a willing distributor cannot onboard a conflicting line until the next review, which lands at or after the end of the venture's Year-1 window. The cell is not small — for the YAM horizon it is closed.

mechanism.text: Master-distributor structures (the MaxPower pattern: one distributor supplying all reseller partners) concentrate line-card decisions into few annual review events, and Legrand's ownership of both Raritan and Server Technology means the intelligent-PDU slots on those line cards are already double-occupied by one incumbent

The venture launches mid-review-cycle (profile: pre-launch, first 12 months); the documented private-label route exists but requires certifications, stock commitments and margin programs a Year-1 entrant cannot yet fund, so no CE distributor can list the product inside the YAM window

cust.distributor (0.13, maturity needs-source, zero corroborating evidence in the ledger) contributes nothing to Year-1 reachable demand; the funnel silently overstates the channel mix available inside the obtainable window

risks.whyMissable: Distribution looks like the LOW-commitment channel — 'anyone can get listed eventually' — and the ledger's 0.13 looks too small to interrogate. The hidden variable is timing: line reviews are annual gates, so a cell that is genuinely open in steady state is closed for precisely the 12-month window the YAM measures. Founders model channel share, not channel calendar.
risks.falsifier: A named CE electrical/IT distributor (Rexel, Sonepar, Also-class) confirming in writing that a new intelligent-PDU line can be onboarded within two quarters without waiting for the annual line review or triggering an exclusivity conflict.

likelihood.rationale: Annual line reviews with incumbency advantages are the documented norm in electrical distribution (base-rate), and the Legrand dual-brand consolidation of the intelligent-PDU shelf is a hard fact; the open question is how rigidly CE distributors enforce review timing for a differentiated product. [evidence revision: The structural premises (master distributors, line reviews, Legrand dual-brand grip) are confirmed, but the decisive timing/lockout premise is contradicted by three separate 2025-2026 cases of new and startup intelligent-PDU vendors being onboarded by European distributors outside any rigid annual-review gate, plus source evidence that reviews are often rolling rather than a single hard cycle. This weakens the 'cell closed for the YAM horizon' conclusion, lowering likelihood from 0.45 toward 0.30 while staying bounded since the incumbency and consolidation facts remain valid friction.]

perturbation.note: Distributor cell unavailable to the venture within the Year-1 horizon — foreclosed by review-cycle timing and shelf exclusivity, not shrunk

risks.title: MID/Eichrecht-grade metering gates the billing use case in Germany — half the geography, the core intelligent-PDU value prop
risks.narrative: The intelligent PDU's premium over dumb power strips rests substantially on outlet- and rack-level metering. But in Germany — 50% of the modeled geography — any meter whose readings are used to bill sub-tenants per measured kWh falls under legal-metrology obligations (MessEG/MID): the metering chain must be conformity-assessed, not merely accurate. A pre-launch entrant whose PDU metering is 'billing-grade' in spec but not MID-certified in law cannot sell the billing use case to German colos at all, and MID module certification is its own multi-quarter, notified-body process that is nowhere on the launch-critical path. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 bundles 'EU cert stack' into one qualitative haircut and never distinguishes this instrument — yet it forecloses the highest-value slice of demand in the largest country cell.

mechanism.text: German legal metrology (MessEG, implementing MID 2014/32/EU) requires conformity-assessed meters wherever measured kWh is billed to third parties — a distinct instrument absent from the ledger's named cert set, which the digest already shows is narrower than the real EU entry stack

Colocation operators re-billing power to tenants are precisely the buyers who pay the intelligent-PDU premium for metering; Germany is 0.50 of the geography split and colocation plus hyperscale wholesale re-billing spans the majority of the segment mix

MID module B+D certification via a notified body is a multi-quarter engineering-plus-audit effort; for a pre-launch venture it cannot complete inside the same Year 1 the funnel is harvesting, so the German billing-metering demand is foreclosed for the period, not merely delayed
serviceableFactor 0.55 silently includes billing-metering demand it cannot legally reach in DE during Year 1; the reachable share sits at the band floor or below until MID certification lands

risks.whyMissable: The product meets or beats billing-class accuracy on its datasheet, so 'metering compliance' looks like a solved engineering problem. Legal metrology is a conformity regime, not an accuracy bar — a distinction invisible until a German colo's procurement lawyer asks for the MID declaration, by which point the tender is lost. And because much colo billing today is capacity-tier rather than per-kWh, early conversations can pass without the gate ever surfacing.
risks.falsifier: Confirmation from two German colocation operators (or the responsible eichamt/PTB guidance) that PDU-level metering used for tenant re-billing is exempt from MessEG conformity requirements in their billing model, or that estimate/capacity-tier billing makes MID-certified PDU metering commercially irrelevant to their purchase decision.

likelihood.rationale: The legal obligation for billed per-kWh metering is clear, but its bite depends on how much CE colo billing is actually measured-kWh at PDU level versus capacity-tier or upstream branch-circuit metering — heterogeneity the corpus documents but does not quantify. [evidence revision: Evidence firmly establishes the legal-metrology gate and the multi-quarter notified-body certification path, and shows PDU-level MID metering is a real, market-recognized colo billing feature (EDP Europe). This strengthens the mechanism's premises above prior. However, the core uncertainty the original rationale flagged — the actual DE colo share billed on measured PDU-level kWh versus capacity-tier/estimate allocation (Vaiking shows estimate-based rack allocation is common) — remains unquantified, capping the upward revision. Also, the serviceableFactor haircut may already partly absorb cert friction, so the marginal foreclosure is bounded.]

risks.title: 2026 demand is already specified: the serviceable haircut ignores that reachability has a clock
risks.narrative: serviceableFactor 0.55 is framed as a channel-and-regulatory reach haircut, as if reach were a static property. But for DC electrical gear, the demand that will invoice in the next 12 months was specified at design stage — the model's own cross-check admits 12-24 month procurement lock, and the corpus shows lock-in occurring far earlier (transformer lead times of 48-60 months, hyperscale cycles up to 4-7 years). Meanwhile the entrant's EU compliance stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, plus EN 50600-compatible documentation) consumes 6-9 months from day 0. The intersection of 'demand contestable in months 1-12' and 'entrant qualified to bid' is a fraction of the static 0.55 — the serviceable share within the YAM window is a different, smaller number than the serviceable share of the market in steady state.

mechanism.text: Rack-power specifications for capacity landing in 2026 were locked at design/procurement stage well before the venture's launch date — the model's own cross-check concedes a 12-24 month lock window, and the growth the market shape describes is precisely this pre-committed pipeline

The entrant cannot bid even the contestable residue until its compliance stack clears: the full EU entry set (WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, RoHS, EORI) is broader than the ledger's named certs and runs 6-9 months, and EN 50600 facility-standard compatibility must be documented for operator RFQs

serviceableFactor 0.55 counts demand that is temporally unreachable within the first 12 months; the effective serviceable share for the YAM window — demand both uncommitted and open to a newly-certified vendor — is closer to 0.35, and the node has no bottom-up cross-check to catch this

risks.whyMissable: The serviceable haircut looks handled because it already discounts 45% of the TAM and cites the regulatory stack. What the static number hides is a timing dimension: 0.55 may be right for the market in general and simultaneously wrong for every month of the venture's first year, because 'reachable' and 'reachable now' are different sets. risks.title: EnEfG §12 + EED Delegated Reg 2024/1364 reporting duties spec-crawl into PDU tenders as certified-accuracy + incumbent-DCIM-integration requirements
risks.narrative: Germany's Energy Efficiency Act obliges data centers to file energy and efficiency data to the Energieeffizienzregister annually (31 March), and the EU's 2024/1364 rating scheme adds a parallel Union-level reporting layer (annual 15 May filings). Neither instrument names PDUs — which is precisely why the model ignores them. But operators discharge these duties through their monitoring chain, so 2026 PDU specifications in Germany and beyond increasingly require certified metering accuracy classes plus validated integration with the incumbent DCIM suites (EcoStruxure, Vertiv Environet, Sunbird) that generate the filings. A new entrant's PDU without a certified accuracy declaration and pre-validated DCIM connectors fails the spec even when its own telemetry is superior — the reporting laws convert the incumbents' software ecosystems into a de facto certification the entrant lacks. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 was set against a product-law cert stack; it does not price an ecosystem-integration gate that binds hardest in the 50% German cell.

mechanism.text: EnEfG and EU 2024/1364 reporting deadlines recur annually through the venture's window, forcing operators to produce auditable energy data — with German colo already publicizing energy metrics (88% RE share) as a compliance posture

Operators push the compliance burden down into procurement specs: certified metering accuracy and named-DCIM integration become mandatory line items in PDU tenders, and the incumbent suites' validation programs (run by the same top-3 vendors) become gatekeepers for challenger hardware

The serviceable share of TAM for an unvalidated entrant sits below the 0.45 band floor in reporting-bound geographies — and the ledger itself concedes the 0.55 has no bottom-up cross-check to catch this

risks.whyMissable: Both instruments bind OPERATORS, not vendors — a founder scanning for laws that name their product finds nothing and files these under 'customer's problem'. The transmission is contractual spec language, not law, so it never appears in a regulatory scan; it appears in lost tenders, one quarter too late.

likelihood.rationale: The reporting obligations and deadlines are enacted; the spec-creep transmission into PDU tenders is plausible and consistent with documented integrator behavior (branded-incumbent selection on DCIM compatibility) but not yet directly evidenced at tender level. risks.narrative: Refinement cycle 1 closed the 'GPU pods skip PDUs' doubt because NVIDIA reference architectures ship PDU variants. But the vendor publishes parallel architectures: the AC/PDU version is the fallback, while the flagship dense design routes power via DC busbar with materially lower PDU attach per rack. The only part of enterprise on-premise that is growing is exactly this GPU-pod build-out; the legacy base is bleeding ~2pp of capacity share per year toward 19% by 2031. So the 0.29 enterprise slice is squeezed twice — the shrinking legacy half keeps buying PDUs it already owns, and the growing AI half is steered toward the substitute. A checkbox marked 'confirmed' is carrying 29% of the TAM.

mechanism.text: Enterprise on-premise capacity growth is now driven almost entirely by GenAI/GPU infrastructure after a sustained no-growth period, and the flagship reference architecture for the densest pods uses DC busbar with sharply reduced rack-PDU attach, with the AC/PDU variant positioned as legacy fallback
