risks.title: Colo-only apportionment plants the flag in Germany/NL while 35–40% of PDU demand sits in non-colo load with a different country mix
risks.narrative: Every geo share divides colo MW by colo MW, but rack-PDU demand tracks TOTAL installed IT load — and the non-colo remainder is not distributed like colocation. Germany's installed load (BMWK >2,700MW) exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW, Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-basis share 0.13 vs 0.08), and Poland's better-corroborated installed-load figure yields 0.10 vs 0.07. EUDCA confirms the gap is continental: colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power. A venture that builds its Year-1 named-account plan on the colo-derived map will staff Frankfurt and Amsterdam while a third of the actual PDU spend — enterprise and self-operated halls in CH, PL, AT — never appears on its target list.

mechanism.text: EUDCA: commercial colo+hyperscale provides just over two-thirds of Europe's IT power — so roughly a third of PDU-consuming load is outside the colo scope every geo.* ratio is built on

The colo-vs-installed divergence is country-specific, not uniform: DE's installed-load method gives 0.42 vs colo 0.50, CH's self-operated stock pushes it to ~0.13 vs 0.08, and PL's triangulated 660MW installed load implies ~0.10 vs 0.07 — the €300M gets reallocated across countries, not just rescaled

The Year-1 plan sells through large operators (colo-centric by construction), so the misallocated map inflates the reachable share of DE/NL and hides demand in CH/PL enterprise halls; the funnel's geography mix shifts against the venture's channel build

risks.whyMissable: Each geo node already discloses its colo-vs-installed crossCheck divergence, so the issue looks acknowledged — but it is acknowledged as per-country BAND WIDTH, not as a correlated, same-direction reallocation across all countries at once. Bands on independent nodes cannot express 'the whole dimension is measured in the wrong denominator'.
risks.falsifier: A single-scope, single-source table of total installed IT load for all CE-7 countries (e.g., EUDCA country profiles) whose per-country shares match the current colo-based shares within ±3 percentage points.

risks.title: Colo-MW geography weights are wrong by construction for the 29% enterprise slice — the Netherlands cell is partly phantom
risks.narrative: Every geo share was apportioned on Statista colocation MW, but the segment axis includes 29% enterprise on-premise demand — and enterprise machine rooms are distributed by industrial base, not by where Amsterdam colo campuses stand. EUDCA's own framing says colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power; the missing third has a different map. NL is the most colo-concentrated market in the set (~70% of its capacity in Amsterdam metro), while Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-base method gives 0.13 vs the curated 0.08) and Germany's installed load exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW. Multiplying colo-weighted geography by an enterprise segment share manufactures enterprise PDU demand in the Netherlands that physically sits in German and Swiss machine rooms. Anyone sizing a DACH enterprise channel off these weights will staff the wrong countries.

mechanism.text: Commercial colocation + hyperscale provide only ~two-thirds of Europe's total IT power — roughly a third of real rack-PDU-consuming load (enterprise on-prem) is invisible to the colo-MW denominator the geo shares were built on

The non-colo third is not distributed like the colo two-thirds: NL capacity is colo-hub concentrated, while CH's installed-base cross-check already runs to 0.13 vs the curated 0.08 and DE's installed load exceeds colo by ~1,000MW; the funnel nonetheless multiplies the same colo-derived geo vector against seg.enterprise = 0.29

For ~29% of segment scope the geo weights systematically overstate NL and understate CH (and DE at the installed-load scope) — geo.NL = 0.28 counts an enterprise slice that is not there, so the NL×enterprise cell is partly phantom and the CH×enterprise cell is undersized

risks.whyMissable: Both axes are individually well-sourced — Statista for geography, Synergy for segment — so each ledger row passes review on its own. The error exists only in the product of the two axes; no single node carries it, no band covers it, and the ledger's colo-vs-installed cross-checks read as per-country noise rather than a systematic per-segment misallocation.
risks.falsifier: A consistent country-level installed-IT-load table (e.g., EUDCA country profiles) showing NL's share of CE-7 total installed load ≈ its colo share (~0.28) — i.e., the non-colo third distributes the same way the colo two-thirds does.

likelihood.rationale: The colo-vs-installed divergence is already documented in the ledger's own cross-checks for DE, CH, AT and PL (all diverging in the direction this hypothesis predicts); only the magnitude and the NL side are unconfirmed. [evidence revision: The trigger premise (colo+hyperscale ≈ two-thirds of IT power, missing third differently distributed) is now directly confirmed by EUDCA's own framing rather than merely inferred, and German installed-vs-colo divergence (>2,700MW installed vs ~1,700MW colo; only 69% colo share) is corroborated by two independent official sources in the predicted direction. risks.title: The €360M ceiling was set with the same contaminated-category trick it claimed to remove — the real base may sit above the band and the plan is sized for the wrong market
risks.narrative: Refinement cycle 1 lowered the band top from €495M to €360M by comparing the BIS ceiling's 21% growth against a '10–12% intelligent-PDU CAGR (IndexBox)'. But that 10–12% figure comes from IndexBox's €8.5–9.5B EU 'Power and Cable Management' report — a category several times broader than rack PDUs — so the correction that purged one contaminated number imported another. Meanwhile Germany alone is forecast at $509.6M in a PDU category by 2031, already exceeding the entire CE-7 base, and the global anchors span $1.7–3.94B, a 2.3x spread wider than the band. If the true CE base sits at €400M+, the failure mode is not embarrassment — it is a Year-1 plan whose certification scope, channel headcount, and manufacturing commitments were sized for €165M of SAM while incumbents scale into the €80–120M the model never showed the board.

mechanism.text: The band-top-lowering comparator ('10–12% intelligent-PDU CAGR, IndexBox') is sourced from the EU Power and Cable Management report — a €8.5–9.5B multi-category basket — not a rack-PDU report, replicating the exact definitional bundling the refinement flagged in the BIS ceiling

Independent evidence points above the band: Germany's broader-PDU forecast alone reaches $509.6M by 2031, and same-category 2025 global anchors span $1.7B to $3.94B — a spread that exceeds the ±20% band and makes the €360M cap an artifact of one contested comparison rather than a bound

With tamBase capped at €360M, SAM and the Year-1 obtainable plan are calibrated low; under-sizing systematically under-provisions certification breadth (serviceable reach) and channel capacity in the launch window, which is unrecoverable in a market where gear is specified 12–24+ months ahead

risks.whyMissable: Downward refinements read as rigor — 'we caught an inflated ceiling and cut it' is exactly what a diligent team wants on the record, so nobody audits the comparator that justified the cut. Upside error also feels safe ('conservative is fine'), hiding that under-sizing misallocates real resources: too-narrow cert scope and too-thin channel for the market that actually exists.
risks.falsifier: The IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report headline (once purchased) landing at or below €360M for the 2025/2026 EU base, confirming the lowered ceiling despite the contaminated comparator.

likelihood.rationale: Multiple broader-category datapoints point above the band, but each carries the same definitional contamination in the upward direction; the honest read is that the ceiling is unproven rather than proven wrong [evidence revision: The trigger premise — that the 10–12% comparator came from the broad €8.5–9.5B Power and Cable Management basket rather than a rack-PDU report — is now confirmed verbatim, and the ~9% CAGR in dedicated rack-PDU sources contradicts the 10–12% figure, strengthening the contamination claim. However, the core falsifier (whether the true EU rack-PDU base actually exceeds €360M) remains untested since dedicated IndexBox rack-PDU headline figures are paywalled; the directional evidence still carries its own category-breadth uncertainty. Modest upward revision from 0.3 to 0.4.]

perturbation.note: Base above the current band top, consistent with the Germany-alone $509.6M signal and the upper global anchors

indicators.signal: Directly-scoped EU/DE rack-PDU headline figures
indicators.where: IndexBox EU Rack PDUs and Germany Rack PDUs reports ($4,000 each); Omdia rack-PDU vendor share table
indicators.threshold: EU headline >€400M or Germany >€180M

indicators.signal: Incumbent EMEA rack-power growth outrunning the model's 9% CAGR
indicators.where: Vertiv and Legrand quarterly earnings calls, EMEA data-center product-line commentary
indicators.threshold: Two consecutive quarters of >15% YoY EMEA rack-power growth

mitigations.action: Commission the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report to fix the base directly — it resolves this hypothesis and the Europe-share hop simultaneously
mitigations.note: The ledger's own promotionPath; a $4,000 spend against a €60M+ band question

mitigations.action: Build the Year-1 operating plan with staged capacity options (certification scope and contract manufacturing volumes expandable at 6-month gates) rather than fixed to the €165M SAM
mitigations.note: Makes the plan robust to the base being above the band without pre-committing capital

specificity.note: Names exact values and moves (€495M→€360M band cut, 10–12% IndexBox comparator, €8.5–9.5B basket, $509.6M Germany, $1.7–3.94B anchor spread), ledger nodes (tamBase, serviceableFactor, obtainableFactor), and priced instruments ($4,000 IndexBox reports). Perturbation and thresholds are concrete numbers, not gestures.

nonObviousness.note: The core claim is not 'the band edge might be wrong' but that the correction itself imported the same definitional contamination it purged — a NEW reason the whyMissable field convincingly argues hides behind the appearance of rigor. Docked one point because half the narrative's upward pressure (wide global anchors, Germany forecast) draws on datapoints a careful ledger reader could already assemble.

mechanismDepth.note: Three-step chain (contaminated comparator → base likely above band → under-provisioned cert scope and channel in a 12–24 month spec-ahead market) with refs at each hop and independently plausible links. The propagation hop is the weak arrow: the risk's own likelihood rationale concedes the upward datapoints 'carry the same definitional contamination,' so 'ceiling unproven' does not fully entail 'base above band.'

evidenceQuality.note: The load-bearing trigger premise — the 10–12% figure comes from the broad €8.5–9.5B basket — is confirmed verbatim by an attached IndexBox source, and Mordor's ~9% rack-PDU CAGR independently contradicts the comparator. But the falsifier premise (EU base >€360M) is untested behind a paywall, and the dedicated rack-PDU stance is 'silent,' which the likelihood rationale honestly admits.

decisionRelevance.note: Directly changes near-term spend and sequencing: a $4,000 information buy against a €60M+ band question and staged capacity gates instead of a fixed €165M-SAM Year-1 plan. Both indicators (report headlines, incumbent EMEA growth) have explicit thresholds and monitoring venues.
